expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: I cannot keep having this same fight with you Ross! Look, maybe we should **take a break**.
Ross: Fine, you're right. Let's take a break, let's cool off...
Rachel: No. A break from us.
[후에]
Rachel: We were on a **break**!
Ross: That, for all I knew would could last forever. That to me is a break-up.
expressions.speaker: Rachel / Ross
expressions.meaning: 관계나 상황에서 잠시 멈추다; 연인 사이에서는 '잠깐 쉬어가자(일시 정지하자)'는 뜻
expressions.nuance: Ross는 'break = break-up(이별)'으로 이해했지만 Rachel은 일시 정지로 생각해 극적인 오해가 생긴다. 연인 관계 외에도 일·습관에서 '잠시 쉬다'는 뜻으로 자연스럽게 쓴다.
expressions.example: I've been stressing about the project for weeks. I need to take a break and clear my head. → 몇 주째 프로젝트 스트레스를 받았어. 잠깐 쉬면서 머리를 식혀야겠어.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Okay, we're gonna take a short break. (Goes over to their table)

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: I don't know, I don't know. Urrrgh! Look, maybe we should take a break.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Yeah, yeah, I was just about to take a break anyways, so...

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: ...let's **cool off**, okay, let's get some frozen yoghurt, or something..
Rachel: No. A break from us.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 흥분이나 화가 가라앉다; 감정을 식히다
expressions.nuance: 'calm down'보다 덜 직접적이고 부드러운 뉘앙스로, '잠깐 각자 시간을 가지며 감정이 식기를 기다리자'는 제안에 자주 쓴다.
expressions.example: Let's take a walk and cool off before we say something we'll regret. → 후회할 말 하기 전에 산책하면서 좀 식히자.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Ooh, this is cool...it says in some parts of the world, people actually eat the placenta. (Joey grimaces)

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Thank you. (Examines it) Oh, cool! Free sample of coffee!

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: How come Richard looks so much cooler with one of these than me?

expressions.dialogue: Joey: (Voice Over) We figured when we couldn't find you, you'd gone home to **make up** with Rachel. Which is probably what you shoulda done.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 싸운 뒤 화해하다
expressions.nuance: 'make it up to someone(보상하다)'과 구별 필요. 'make up'만 쓰면 화해하다, 'make up with 사람'처럼 대상을 붙일 수도 있다.
expressions.example: After the argument, they finally made up and went out for dinner together. → 다툰 후 결국 화해하고 함께 저녁을 먹으러 갔다.

occ.speaker: Mr. Geller
occ.text: 'Cause there's time to make up for that. We can do stuff together. You always wanted to go to that Colonial Williamsburg. How 'bout we do that?

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Whoa, whoa, whoa. What makes you think we're gonna break up?

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: OK, listen, I'm sorry about last night and I really want to make it up to you.

expressions.dialogue: Rachel: (on answering machine) Hi, it's me. I've been trying to **reach** you all night. I feel awful.
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 누군가에게 연락을 취하려 하다
expressions.nuance: 'contact'보다 구어적이며, 전화·문자·이메일 등 여러 수단을 동원했으나 연결이 안 된 상황에 자연스럽다. 'get hold of someone'과 비슷한 뉘앙스.
expressions.example: I've been trying to reach my professor all day but he's not picking up. → 교수님께 하루 종일 연락하려 했는데 전화를 안 받으셔.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Interesting. all right I'll go out with her again and try to get past it (reaches for the chips) OH SALT BLOATY!

expressions.dialogue: Rachel: (on answering machine) ...I don't want to **get back together** over a machine. So, I love you.
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 헤어졌던 연인이 다시 사귀다; 관계를 재개하다
expressions.nuance: 'over a machine'은 '기계(자동응답기)를 통해서'라는 뜻으로, 직접 만나서 하고 싶다는 의미. 'get together'가 처음 만남이라면 'get back together'는 재결합.
expressions.example: They broke up last year but just got back together after months apart. → 작년에 헤어졌는데 몇 달 만에 다시 사귀기 시작했어.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Ok. Well, before I say anything, I just need to know, is this one of those things where you break up with a guy, and then I tell you what I think, and then the next day you get back together with the guy, and I look like a complete idiot?

occ.speaker: Leslie
occ.text: Well, I y'know, I was just, umm, I was just thinking and hoping, that umm, maybe you'd want to get back together?

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: (in a deep voice, imitating Ross) Um, Rachel I'm really sorry. (imitating Rachel) That's okay, do you wanna get back together? (imitating Ross) Yeah, okay. (in her normal voice) Did anyone else hear that?!

expressions.dialogue: Rachel: (on answering machine) I've been trying to reach you all night. I **feel awful**. Please, Ross, you gotta know there is nothing between me and Mark.
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 매우 미안하거나 죄책감을 느끼다; 감정적으로 몹시 괴롭다
expressions.nuance: 몸이 아플 때도 쓰지만 이 문맥처럼 '너무 미안하다/마음이 괴롭다'는 감정 표현으로도 자주 쓴다. 'feel terrible'과 거의 동의어.
expressions.example: I forgot her birthday completely. I feel awful about it. → 그녀 생일을 완전히 잊어버렸어. 정말 미안하고 괴로워.

occ.speaker: Joanna
occ.text: A couple of hours, I feel awful.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: I just feel awful.

expressions.dialogue: Ross: (on the phone) I've been thinking, this is crazy, I mean don't, don't you think we can **work on this**?
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 문제·관계 등을 개선하기 위해 노력하다
expressions.nuance: 관계에 쓸 때는 '포기하지 않고 함께 해결해 나가다'는 의지의 뉘앙스. 단순히 'fix(고치다)'보다 과정과 지속적인 노력을 강조한다.
expressions.example: We have our differences, but I think we can work on this if we're both willing. → 우리 사이에 차이가 있지만, 둘 다 노력한다면 개선할 수 있을 것 같아.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: You must stop! (Chandler hits what he is working on with a hammer and it collapses.)

occ.speaker: Janice
occ.text: (holding the full cup) No, no, I'm still working on mine.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: What 'not work out'? I'm seeing her again on Thursday. Didn't you listen to the story?

expressions.dialogue: Joey: Look, Ross look, I'm **on board** about this totally honesty thing, I am, just not about stuff that's gonna get you in trouble.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 어떤 아이디어나 계획에 동의하거나 지지하다
expressions.nuance: 배에 올라탔다는 이미지에서 온 표현. 팀·프로젝트·아이디어에 '나도 찬성이야/같이 할게'라는 뜻으로, 비즈니스 회의에서도 자주 쓰인다.
expressions.example: Everyone's on board with the new schedule except for the marketing team. → 마케팅 팀 빼고는 다들 새 일정에 동의했어.

occ.speaker: David
occ.text: Uh, that's definitely a, uh, valid question. And, uh, the answer would be (Writes YES on the board) yes. Yes I was. But, see, I wanted it to be this phenomenal kiss that happened at this phenomenal moment, because, well, 'cause it's you.

occ.speaker: Gate Agent
occ.text: (On the P.A.) All ticketed passengers for Flight 1066 to Athens should now be on board.

occ.speaker: Gate Agent
occ.text: (On the P.A.) All ticketed passengers for Flight 1066 to Athens should now be on board.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: All right look, if you absolutely have to tell her, at least wait until the timing's right. And **that's what deathbeds are for**.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: '~은 바로 그럴 때(그 용도로) 쓰는 거야'라는 뜻의 구문
expressions.nuance: Chandler의 블랙 유머가 담긴 대사이지만, 'That's what X is/are for' 구문 자체는 무언가의 용도나 존재 이유를 강조할 때 일상에서도 폭넓게 쓰인다.
expressions.example: Don't worry about making mistakes — that's what practice is for. → 실수를 걱정하지 마, 연습이란 게 바로 그럴 때 있는 거잖아.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Are you kidding? That's what sisters are for.

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: All right, okay, now, we just have to make sure she doesn't find out some other way. Did you **think about the trail**?
Ross: What trail?
Joey: The trail from the woman you did it with to the woman you hope never finds out! You always have to think about the trail!
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 소문이 퍼질 수 있는 사람들의 연결 고리(경로)를 생각하다
expressions.nuance: 정보나 소문이 A→B→C로 전달되는 경로를 'trail(흔적·경로)'에 비유한 Joey식 표현. 비밀을 숨겨야 할 때 '누가 누구에게 말할 수 있는지' 추적하는 개념으로 쓰인다.
expressions.example: Before you vent to your coworker, think about the trail — she's close with your manager. → 동료에게 털어놓기 전에 소문 경로를 생각해봐. 그 사람 팀장이랑 친하잖아.

expressions.dialogue:
Issac: **You dog!**
Ross: Yes, I suppose I am a dog. But Issac, see I happen to have a girlfriend.
expressions.speaker: Issac
expressions.meaning: 성적으로 대담한 행동을 한 남자에게 놀라움·감탄 반응으로 쓰는 표현
expressions.nuance: 부정적 의미의 '개 같은 놈'이 아니라, 친구의 연애·성적 일탈에 '야, 이 녀석 봐라!'는 뉘앙스. 1990년대 미국 구어에서 흔했으며 현재는 다소 구식이나 드라마·영화에 자주 등장한다.
expressions.example: You asked her out the same day you met her? You dog! → 처음 만난 날 바로 데이트 신청했어? 이 녀석 봐라!

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: (to Ross) Are, are you okay? I mean, do you want me to stay?

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: So what are you gonna do?

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Yeah, it's like when you're a kid, and your parents put your dog to sleep, and they tell you it went off to live on some farm.

expressions.dialogue: Issac: Aw, no problem dude. Y'know we got to **look out for each other**. We're the same, you and me.
expressions.speaker: Issac
expressions.meaning: 서로를 돌봐주다; 서로의 편이 되어 보호하다
expressions.nuance: 'look out for'는 '경계하다'와 '돌보다' 두 의미가 있다. 여기서는 비밀 유지로 서로를 보호한다는 남성 연대 뉘앙스로 쓰였다.
expressions.example: We're a team — we look out for each other no matter what. → 우리는 한 팀이야. 무슨 일이 있어도 서로 챙기는 거야.

expressions.dialogue: Joey: Yeah, I think that women just have a **lower threshold of pain** than men, that's all. I mean, come on, it's just a little wax.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 고통(또는 무언가)을 견디는 한계점이 낮다/높다
expressions.nuance: 'threshold'는 '한계점·임계점'으로, 'threshold of pain' 외에도 'stress threshold(스트레스 한계)', 'noise threshold(소음 인내 한계)' 등으로 확장해 쓸 수 있다.
expressions.example: She has a really high threshold for spicy food — she can handle anything. → 걔는 매운 음식 내성이 정말 강해서 뭐든 다 먹어.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Please I have an extremely high threshold...Holly Mother Of God! My face! My face!! I'm all right! I'm all right! Just a little bit of shock that's all but I'll be fine you can go again. I'm OK (He tries to avoid the tweezers) Dammit! Woman!! How Hoooow!

expressions.expression: have a hell of a time (ironic/sarcastic)
expressions.dialogue: Rachel: Well, you sure **had a hell of a time** at the wake!
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: (반어적으로) 슬프거나 진지해야 할 상황에서 오히려 신나게 즐겼다고 비꼬는 표현
expressions.nuance: 'have a hell of a time'은 원래 '엄청 즐거운 시간을 보내다'는 뜻이지만, Rachel은 Ross가 '관계의 장례식 날 밤(wake)'에 다른 여자와 잠자리를 한 것을 꼬집는 반어로 썼다.
expressions.example: So you forgot our anniversary and you sure had a hell of a time with your friends that night. → 우리 기념일 잊어버리고 그날 밤 친구들이랑 신나게 놀았겠네, 참.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: (sarcastic) Oh, she's sorry! I feel better!

occ.speaker: Receptionist
occ.text: (sarcastic) Any minute now.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: (sarcastically) And you didn't marry him because...?

expressions.expression: get out of ~ on a technicality
expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: You think you're gonna **get out of this on a technicality**?
Ross: Look, I'm not trying to get out of anything, okay. I thought our relationship was dead!
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 사소한 논리적 허점이나 규정 구실로 책임에서 빠져나오려 하다
expressions.nuance: 법률 용어('technicality = 절차상의 기술적 이유')에서 나온 표현으로, 일상에서는 '핑계·구실로 책임을 피하려 한다'는 비난의 뉘앙스로 쓴다.
expressions.example: He admitted the project failed but tried to get out of it on a technicality, claiming he wasn't officially in charge. → 프로젝트 실패를 인정하면서도 공식 담당자가 아니었다는 논리로 책임을 피하려 했어.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: You're the one that bailed on us. You're the one that ran when things got just a little rough!
Rachel: That's....
Ross: That's what?!
Rachel: That is **neither here nor there**.
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 지금 논의와 관련 없다; 핵심에서 벗어난 얘기다
expressions.nuance: 논점을 피하거나 특정 주제를 현재 대화에서 제외시킬 때 쓰는 표현. Rachel이 Ross의 반격을 인정하기 싫어 주제를 돌리는 데서 그 회피적 뉘앙스가 잘 드러난다.
expressions.example: Whether the idea was originally mine is neither here nor there — what matters is whether it works. → 아이디어가 원래 내 것인지는 중요하지 않아. 효과가 있는지가 핵심이지.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Yes. Well, that's neither here nor there.

expressions.dialogue: Ross: Now we're in a tough spot again, Rach. What do you want to do? Do you wanna **fight for us**? Or, do you wanna bail?
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 포기하지 않고 (관계·목표 등을) 지키기 위해 싸우다; 끝까지 노력하다
expressions.nuance: 연인 관계에서 'fight for us/our relationship'은 '우리를 지키기 위해 싸우다'는 강한 의지 표현. 직장·꿈·신념 등에도 폭넓게 쓰인다.
expressions.example: She wasn't ready to give up. She decided to fight for her position at the company. → 그녀는 포기하지 않기로 했다. 회사에서 자신의 자리를 지키기 위해 싸우기로.

occ.speaker: Announcer
occ.text: From New York City, New York! Appearing in his Ultimate Fighting Championship debut! He's known for his confrontational business style. Ladies and Gentlemen, introducing Pete Beck-errrr!!

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Yeah, you have to go fight for her!

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Y'know I was thinking if we had a...a big fight and uh we broke up for a few hours...

expressions.dialogue: Ross: ...you're the one that **bailed on us**. You're the one that ran when things got just a little rough!
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 어렵거나 불편한 상황에서 갑자기 떠나버리다; 약속·관계를 저버리다
expressions.nuance: 'bail out'은 '위기에서 구해주다'이지만 'bail on someone'은 그 반대로 '상대를 버리고 떠나다'의 부정적 의미. 구어에서 매우 흔하게 쓰인다.
expressions.example: He said he'd come to the party but bailed on us at the last minute. → 파티에 온다고 했다가 마지막 순간에 그냥 빠져버렸어.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: No, no, no, don't you dare bail on me. The only reason she's goin' out with me is because I said I could bring a friend for her friend.

expressions.dialogue: Ross: I just can't see us **throwing away** something we know is so damn good. Rachel, I love you so much.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 가치 있는 것을 낭비하거나 무의미하게 포기하다
expressions.nuance: 쓰레기를 버리듯 소중한 것을 아깝게 날리다는 이미지. 'waste'보다 더 아깝고 안타깝다는 감정이 실린다.
expressions.example: Don't throw away five years of friendship over one misunderstanding. → 한 번의 오해로 5년 우정을 날려버리지 마.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Hey Ross. This probably isn't the best time to bring it up, but you have to throw a party for Monica.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Pathetic mess? I know, but-come on, man, she's needy, she's vulnerable. I'm thinkin', cha-ching! (Rachel throws a roll at Joey. He picks it up and eats it.) Thanks. Look, you have not been out with a woman since Janice. You're doin' this.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: ...teen! (throws in a ten-dollar bill)

expressions.dialogue: Ross: Look, there's got to be a way we can **work past this**. Okay, I can't imagine my life without you.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 문제나 장애물을 극복하고 앞으로 나아가다
expressions.nuance: 'work through(깊이 파고들어 해결하다)'와 비슷하지만, 'work past'는 그 문제를 '넘어서다'는 뉘앙스가 강하다. 관계, 트라우마, 갈등 극복에 자주 쓰인다.
expressions.example: It took a while, but they were able to work past the betrayal and rebuild trust. → 시간이 걸렸지만, 그들은 배신을 딛고 신뢰를 다시 쌓을 수 있었다.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: That's what you've been working on for the past two hours?!

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Come on sweetie! You've had to work late every night for the past two weeks, what is it this time?

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: (smiling) Now let's not ruin this day. You worked so hard. Let's move past this and try to have a nice meal all together, huh?

expressions.dialogue: Rachel: No Ross!! You can't just kiss me and think you're gonna **make it all go away**, okay? It doesn't work that way.
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 문제·감정·상처를 없애버리다; 없었던 것처럼 만들다
expressions.nuance: 상대방이 쉬운 방법(사과·키스 등)으로 심각한 문제를 해결하려 할 때 '그렇게 간단히 해결될 문제가 아니다'라는 반박에 자주 쓰인다.
expressions.example: Saying sorry doesn't just make it all go away. You need to show you've actually changed. → 미안하다는 말 한마디로 다 해결되는 게 아니야. 정말 달라졌다는 걸 보여줘야 해.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Oh my God, it's gonna WORK! We're gonna make it work! I'm gonna be a mummy and (to Chandler) you're gonna be a daddy! All right, I'll see you suckers. I'm gonna get me... A BABY! {she leaves)

expressions.dialogue: Rachel: You can't just kiss me and think you're gonna make it all go away, okay? **It doesn't work that way**. It doesn't just make it better.
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 그런 식으로는 되지 않는다; 그게 그렇게 단순하지 않다
expressions.nuance: 상대방의 단순한 해결책이나 논리에 '현실은 그렇게 돌아가지 않아'라고 반박할 때 쓰는 만능 표현. 다양한 상황에 바로 적용할 수 있다.
expressions.example: You can't just apologize and expect everything to go back to normal. It doesn't work that way. → 사과 한 번 하고 다 원래대로 돌아가길 바라는 건 아니지? 그렇게 되는 게 아니거든.

expressions.dialogue: Rachel: ...it doesn't matter what you say, or what you do, Ross. I just **can't stop picturing** [you] with her, I can't...
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: ~하는 것을 멈출 수 없다; 의지와 상관없이 자꾸만 ~하게 되다
expressions.nuance: 의지와 무관하게 머릿속에서 반복되는 이미지나 감정을 표현할 때 쓴다. 'picture(그려지다)', 'think about', 'replay' 등과의 조합이 특히 자연스럽다.
expressions.example: I can't stop thinking about what she said. It keeps replaying in my head. → 그녀가 한 말이 자꾸 머릿속에 맴돌아. 멈출 수가 없어.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: (at the urinal) Can you stop yellin'? You're makin' me nervous, and I can't go when I'm nervous.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Uhh, I can't stop thinking about it. It's just so hard. I just wanna grab all these houses and rub 'em all over my body. [Grabs a handful of the houses.]

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: I do! So much! I can't stop thinking about her! I can't sleep, I-

expressions.dialogue: Rachel: And yet she was worth **jeopardising** our relationship!!
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 소중한 것을 위험에 빠뜨리다; 망칠 위험을 감수하다
expressions.nuance: 'risk'보다 더 격식 있고 감정적 무게감이 있는 단어. 관계(relationship), 경력(career), 명성(reputation)처럼 잃으면 안 되는 것과 주로 함께 쓰인다.
expressions.example: One careless post on social media could jeopardize your entire career. → SNS에 부주의한 게시물 하나가 커리어 전체를 위험에 빠뜨릴 수 있어.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: (pouring two glasses) Hey look Ross, you need to understand something okay? I uh...I am never gonna act on this Rachel thing, okay? I-I would never do anything to jeopardize my friendship with you. (He hands Ross a glass.)

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: That's ok. Uh, anyway, well he and I both really liked you a lot, uhm, but we didn't want anything to jeopardize our friendship, so we kinda made a pact, that neither of us could ask you out!

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: Hey. It's three in the morning. They don't know that I've come home yet. You notice how neither one of them are wondering where I am.
Phoebe: Yeah, y'know, **people can be so self-involved**.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 자기 자신에게만 몰두해 있다; 자기중심적이다
expressions.nuance: 'selfish(이기적인)'보다 순하지만, 다른 사람의 감정이나 상황에 무관심하다는 뜻을 담고 있다. 직접 비난보다 살짝 거리를 두고 관찰하는 어조로 쓸 때 자연스럽다.
expressions.example: He never asks how you're doing. He's so self-involved it's exhausting. → 걘 네가 어떻게 지내는지 절대 안 물어봐. 워낙 자기 생각만 해서 같이 있으면 지쳐.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: So. This has always been Monica's bed, what you're just noticing now, how self-involved are you?
